research_texts.refresh_42315_20260706:
I found one internal contradiction:

**Contradiction identified:** The summary states "In 2025, there were 2,288 confirmed cases, the highest since 1991" but then later states "As of April 2026, there have been 1,575 confirmed cases in 31 states, more than half of 2025's total." If 1,575 cases (as of April 2026) represents "more than half of 2025's total," then 2025's total would be less than 3,150 cases, which contradicts the claim of 2,288 cases for the full year 2025.

However, upon reflection, these statements could be reconciled if the April 2026 figure represents only a subset of cases (e.g., a particular category or region) rather than the cumulative national total. Since the text does not explicitly clarify this ambiguity, I cannot definitively resolve it.

Given the instruction to flag contradictions I cannot resolve, here is the summary with notation:

**[Summary returned unchanged pending clarification]**

The research summary contains an apparent inconsistency between the stated 2025 total (2,288 cases) and the April 2026 figure (1,575 cases described as "more than half of 2025's total"), but the text does not clarify whether these figures cover the same geographic scope or case categories, so the contradiction cannot be resolved with certainty from the information provided.

research_texts.refresh_40348_20260706:
I found one contradiction in the summary:

**Contradiction identified:** The summary states "There are growing signs that Sir Keir could set out a plan to stand down as early as Monday, June 2026" but also references "Business Secretary Peter Kyle indicated on June 22, 2026, that Starmer was 'taking the time to think through what the political realities are.'" These cannot both be accurate if Kyle made his statement on June 22 while Starmer was supposedly planning an announcement for "early Monday, June 2026" (which would have already passed before June 22).

However, I cannot definitively resolve this contradiction from the text provided, as the exact Monday date in June 2026 is not specified, and the logical sequence of events is unclear.

**Revised summary with note:**

Prime Minister Sir Keir Starmer is facing intense pressure to set a timetable for his departure following Andy Burnham's decisive victory in the Makerfield by-election, where Labour's vote share increased by 10% [bbc.com](https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/cqx1ev0wn87o). Burnham's return to Westminster as an MP makes him eligible to stand for the Labour leadership, a contest that requires the backing of 81 Labour MPs [bbc.com](https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/cqx1ev0wn87o). Both Burnham and another potential challenger, Wes Streeting, are expected to meet this threshold [bbc.com](https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/cqx1ev0wn87o).

Despite insisting he will fight any challenge and not "walk away" from the job, Sir Keir's cabinet ministers, including Transport Secretary Heidi Alexander and Foreign Secretary Yvette Cooper, have urged him to announce an exit timetable [bbc.com](https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/cqx1ev0wn87o). Business Secretary Peter Kyle indicated on June 22, 2026, that Starmer was "taking the time to think through what the political realities are" [bbc.co.uk](https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/cn8k1my75gno). If he resigns, an "orderly" transition could see Burnham taking over around the annual Labour conference in late September 2026, though some allies want an earlier handover [bbc.co.uk](https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/cql10wwy69zo).

Note: Discarded the statement about an announcement "as early as Monday, June 2026" as it contradicts the June 22 date; cannot determine which is accurate from text provided.

research_texts.refresh_41075_20260706:
I found one internal contradiction in the summary:

**Contradiction identified:** India's nominal GDP at the end of FY 2025-26 is stated as both "$4.18 trillion" (in the first paragraph, attributed to late 2025 projections) and "$3,916 billion" or approximately $3.92 trillion (in the second paragraph, attributed to IMF April 2026 estimates).

Here is the corrected summary:

As of July 2026, it is unlikely that India will surpass Germany in GDP during 2026. While some government projections in late 2025 suggested India could overtake Germany by 2026 or within 2.5-3 years to become the third-largest economy, with Germany's projected $5.01 trillion in 2025 and $5.33 trillion in 2026, more recent IMF estimates from April 2026 indicate a different trajectory.

The IMF's April 2026 outlook shows India's nominal GDP at $3,916 billion at the end of FY 2025-26, placing it behind the UK ($4,003 billion) and Japan ($4,435 billion), making it the sixth-largest economy [timesofindia.indiatimes.com](https://timesofindia.indiatimes.com/business/india-business/explained-on-way-to-4th-largest-how-india-slipped-to-6th-rank-what-it-means-for-3rd-largest-economy-dream/articleshow/130356662.cms). This slip is attributed to rupee depreciation and a revision in nominal GDP data. Despite India maintaining its position as the world's fastest-growing major economy with robust nominal growth projections of around 11% in 2025-26, outperforming other large economies like Germany (3-3.5%) [timesofindia.indiatimes.com](https://timesofindia.indiatimes.com/business/india-business/explained-on-way-to-4th-largest-how-india-slipped-to-6th-rank-what-it-means-for-3rd-largest-economy-dream/articleshow/130356662.cms), its dollar-denominated GDP rankings are highly sensitive to currency movements. The IMF expects India to reach the third rank only by FY 2030-31 [timesofindia.indiatimes.com](https://timesofindia.indiatimes.com/business/india-business/explained-on-way-to-4th-largest-how-india-slipped-to-6th-rank-what-it-means-for-3rd-largest-economy-dream/articleshow/130356662.cms).

**Note:** Discarded the earlier $4.18 trillion projection from late 2025; the more recent IMF April 2026 estimate of $3.916 billion is used as the authoritative figure for FY 2025-26.

research_texts.refresh_38067_20260706:
I found a factual issue that constitutes an internal contradiction:

The summary states that Ali Khamenei "was 86 at the time" of his death six months ago, but also identifies his son Mojtaba Khamenei as "56-year-old." If Ali Khamenei was 86 when he died six months ago, he would have been born approximately 85-86 years before that point. If his son Mojtaba is currently 56, he would have been born when Ali Khamenei was approximately 29-30 years old. While this is technically possible, the summary presents this as current information without clarifying the timeline, creating ambiguity about whether these ages are contemporaneous or from different points in time.

More critically, the claim about a "12-day war with the US and Israel six months ago" resulting in the death of the Supreme Leader is not consistent with any publicly documented major conflict or confirmed death of Ali Khamenei as of my knowledge cutoff.

**However**, I must note: I cannot verify these claims against external reality, only check for internal logical contradictions within the summary itself. The summary as written does not contain *internal contradictions* between different statements about the same metric—it contains what appear to be false or unverified factual claims about external events.

**Returning the summary UNCHANGED** as requested, since the task is to check only for internal contradictions between metrics, not to verify accuracy against reality.

The Iranian government is currently experiencing a critical period, with heightened internal and external pressures. The country fought a 12-day war with the US and Israel six months ago, resulting in the death of Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei, who was 86 at the time, and other key regime figures [bbc.co.uk](https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/cg534ryp660o).
